hypotheses.statement: Three.js + R3F is the best choice due to largest React ecosystem and proven production track record
architectureImplications: Need separate WS server for multiplayer

hypotheses.statement: PlayCanvas is the better choice due to native WebGPU + visual editor + React support enabling faster prototyping

scenarios.name: R3F Base: WebGL fallback on Chromebooks
scenarios.description: Three.js + R3F with WebGL2 rendering, Colyseus for multiplayer on Railway/Fly.io, Vercel for static + API
assumptions: A1: WebGL2 achieves 30fps on 2023 Intel Chromebooks
A2: Colyseus handles 30 concurrent users per room
A3: Vercel CDN delivers static assets under 2s globally
architectureImplications: Separate WS hosting adds ops complexity
Rich drei ecosystem reduces custom code
governanceImplications: Client-side rendering may avoid COPPA if no personal data collected
safetyImplications: No student PII stored server-side in base case
recommendedActions: Prototype with R3F + drei, measure Chromebook FPS

evaluationScores.rationale: R3F docs + Vercel docs directly confirm core claims
Well-documented stack, create-r3f-app template exists
WebGL fallback is proven, low risk
Three.js is standard — components reusable
React devs productive immediately via JSX
Client-side rendering avoids most COPPA triggers
Three.js: 10+ years, R3F: 4+ years, largest 3D web community

scenarios.name: R3F Best: WebGPU on modern Chromebooks, 60fps
scenarios.description: WebGPU enabled on Chrome 113+, 60fps rendering, physics via rapier, accessibility via @react-three/a11y
assumptions: A1: School Chromebooks have Chrome 113+
A2: WebGPU gives 2x perf over WebGL
A3: @react-three/a11y meets WCAG for interactive 3D
architectureImplications: Can use more complex scenes with physics
deploymentImplications: Same as base — Vercel + external WS
safetyImplications: Reduced seizure risk with controlled animations
recommendedActions: Test WebGPU on actual Chromebook hardware

evaluationScores.rationale: WebGPU on Chromebook not directly tested — inference from Chrome version
Same stack as base, WebGPU is transparent via Three.js
WebGL fallback exists if WebGPU unavailable
Same reversibility as base
No extra work — Three.js auto-selects renderer
Same as base + a11y bonus
Identical ecosystem to base

scenarios.name: R3F Worst: Chromebook GPU too weak for complex scenes
scenarios.description: Low-end ARM Chromebooks can't maintain 30fps with physics + particles, requires aggressive LOD
assumptions: A1: Intel Celeron/ARM Chromebooks have limited GPU
A2: Complex math visualizations need particles/physics
A3: LOD implementation adds development time
contradictions: WebGL is proven on mobile — but educational 3D may demand more
architectureImplications: May need to reduce visual fidelity
deploymentImplications: Same hosting but heavier CDN asset load
governanceImplications: Reduced interactivity may lower engagement below educational threshold
recommendedActions: Exp-01: Chromebook perf test BEFORE committing

evaluationScores.rationale: No direct Chromebook GPU benchmark data available
LOD + optimization adds weeks of work
Could block the project if perf is unusable
Can switch to simpler visuals but loses educational value
Optimization delays MVP by weeks
Reduced functionality may not meet educational standards
Ecosystem unchanged by device limitations

scenarios.name: R3F Adversarial: Colyseus scaling + COPPA audit failure
scenarios.description: Multiplayer server becomes bottleneck at school-wide deployment. COPPA audit reveals student tracking requires consent.
assumptions: A1: School deploys to 500+ students simultaneously
A2: Progress tracking constitutes personal information
A3: Colyseus free tier has connection limits
contradictions: COPPA scope depends on data collection specifics
architectureImplications: May need to anonymize all student data
deploymentImplications: Colyseus scaling requires paid tier or self-hosting
safetyImplications: Student data exposure if not properly anonymized

evaluationScores.rationale: COPPA rules are clear; application to this specific case needs legal review
Anonymization + consent flow adds significant work
COPPA violation = regulatory action, project shutdown
Data architecture decisions are hard to reverse
Legal review + consent UI delays launch
Without COPPA audit, compliance is uncertain
Ecosystem unaffected

scenarios.name: PlayCanvas Base: WebGPU native + visual editor
scenarios.description: PlayCanvas engine + editor for 3D content, PartyKit for edge multiplayer, Vercel for static
assumptions: A1: PlayCanvas React integration is production-quality
A2: PartyKit handles 30 users at edge latency
A3: Visual editor accelerates educational content creation
contradictions: React support mentioned on homepage but depth unclear
architectureImplications: Visual editor reduces developer dependency for content
governanceImplications: Same COPPA considerations as Option A
recommendedActions: Verify React integration depth via PlayCanvas docs

evaluationScores.rationale: Homepage mentions React but detailed docs not fetched
Editor helps but smaller community = less Stack Overflow help
Less proven for React educational apps
PlayCanvas-specific assets may need rework
Visual editor could accelerate content creation
Same as Option A
Smaller than Three.js but growing, WebGPU-first

scenarios.name: PlayCanvas Best: Editor + WebGPU + edge multiplayer
scenarios.description: Visual editor enables non-developers to create math lessons. WebGPU delivers 60fps. PartyKit scales at edge.
assumptions: A1: Teachers can use visual editor to create content
A2: WebGPU performance is 2x WebGL on Chromebooks
A3: PartyKit edge deployment = sub-50ms latency
architectureImplications: Content creation pipeline independent of engineering
safetyImplications: User-generated content risk if teachers create lessons
recommendedActions: Prototype editor workflow with sample math lesson

evaluationScores.rationale: Teacher editor workflow is aspirational — no evidence of educational use
Editor simplifies 3D but custom logic still needed
Low risk if prototype validates
Editor-dependent assets less portable
Visual editor = fastest prototyping path
UGC review process needed
Growing but less proven for education

scenarios.name: PlayCanvas Worst: React integration shallow, community sparse
scenarios.description: React support is superficial (web component wrapper), not a true renderer. Limited educational 3D examples.
assumptions: A1: React support is web component wrapper, not R3F-level
A2: Few PlayCanvas educational examples exist
A3: Custom educational components require engine-level knowledge
contradictions: Homepage says React but may be shallow integration
architectureImplications: May need to write custom React bindings
deploymentImplications: Same hosting but more custom code
governanceImplications: Less community review of educational patterns
recommendedActions: Exp: Build one React component with PlayCanvas and measure DX

evaluationScores.rationale: React depth unknown — homepage claim not verified in detail
Custom bindings = high effort if needed
Could block React-first development workflow
PlayCanvas-specific code harder to migrate
Custom bindings delay MVP significantly
Compliance unaffected by tech choice
Sparse educational examples

scenarios.name: PlayCanvas Adversarial: Editor vendor dependency
scenarios.description: PlayCanvas editor becomes a critical dependency. Editor changes break lesson content. Migration is expensive.
assumptions: A1: Editor is the primary content creation tool
A2: Editor API changes break existing lessons
A3: No standard export format for 3D educational content
safetyImplications: No safety risk from editor dependency
recommendedActions: Build content pipeline that doesn't require editor

evaluationScores.rationale: Vendor lock-in is a known pattern for editor-centric tools
Migration from editor-locked content is expensive
Content pipeline blocked by vendor changes
Editor-dependent content is hard to extract
Initially fast but lock-in costs compound
No compliance impact
MIT license mitigates but editor dependency remains

scenarios.name: Babylon.js Base: Native WebGPU + PartyKit
scenarios.description: Babylon.js 7.x with native WebGPU, React-Babylon.js wrapper, PartyKit for multiplayer
assumptions: A1: react-babylonjs provides adequate React integration
A2: Babylon.js WebGPU renderer is production-stable
A3: Community is sufficient for educational 3D patterns
contradictions: Babylon.js docs not directly fetched in this run
deploymentImplications: Same as Option B: Vercel + PartyKit
recommendedActions: Fetch Babylon.js docs to resolve evidence gap

evaluationScores.rationale: Babylon.js docs not directly fetched — evidence gap
React wrapper exists but less mature than R3F
Unknown risk due to evidence gap
Standard WebGL/WebGPU — portable
Less React tooling = slower start
Same compliance profile
Mature engine, smaller web ecosystem than Three.js

scenarios.name: Babylon.js Best: WebGPU native perf advantage
scenarios.description: Native WebGPU gives Babylon.js 2x performance advantage on modern Chromebooks.
assumptions: A1: WebGPU perf difference is material (2x)
A2: Chromebooks have Chrome 113+
A3: Native WebGPU > Three.js WebGPU adapter
architectureImplications: Best GPU utilization of all options
recommendedActions: Exp-01 variant: benchmark Babylon vs Three on Chromebook

evaluationScores.rationale: No benchmark data — pure inference
Inference from general Babylon.js knowledge
Unknown without benchmarks
Same as base
Same as base
Same
Mature engine

scenarios.name: Babylon.js Worst: React integration friction
scenarios.description: react-babylonjs wrapper has gaps, forcing vanilla JS for complex interactions. Developer experience suffers.
assumptions: A1: react-babylonjs doesn't cover all Babylon APIs
A2: Team is React-first
A3: Mixing vanilla + React creates maintenance burden
safetyImplications: More complex code = more potential bugs
recommendedActions: Test react-babylonjs with educational interaction patterns

evaluationScores.rationale: No direct evidence fetched for react-babylonjs quality
Hybrid codebase adds complexity
DX friction could slow entire team
Can migrate to R3F but expensive
Wrapper gaps = workarounds needed
Same
Smaller React ecosystem for Babylon

scenarios.name: Babylon.js Adversarial: WebGPU browser fragmentation
scenarios.description: WebGPU spec changes break Babylon.js implementation. Firefox never ships WebGPU. Safari support lags.
assumptions: A1: WebGPU spec is not yet finalized across all browsers
A2: Schools may have Firefox or Safari
A3: Babylon.js WebGPU-first strategy amplifies browser fragmentation risk
deploymentImplications: Testing across browsers adds CI complexity
governanceImplications: Accessibility: must work on all school browsers
recommendedActions: Verify school browser inventory before committing to WebGPU-first

evaluationScores.rationale: Browser support data partially available but not comprehensive
Dual renderer maintenance is real cost
Browser fragmentation could block deployment
WebGL fallback exists
Cross-browser testing slows release
Must ensure all students can access
WebGPU ecosystem still maturing

simulationRuns.result: 12 scenarios generated, scored, 2 revision rounds completed

contradictionsAddressed: PlayCanvas React claim resolved — homepage says React support but depth unclear → marked resolved with caveat
revisionRounds.summary: Round 1: Resolved PlayCanvas/React contradiction. PlayCanvas homepage confirms React but integration depth is uncertain. sc-b-base downgraded Evidence Fit to 3. sc-b-worst added as explicit shallow-integration scenario.

contradictionsAddressed: Babylon.js evidence gap flagged as detected — cannot resolve without fetching docs
revisionRounds.summary: Round 2: Babylon.js scenarios retain contradictionStatus=detected for base and best cases due to unfetched docs. Evidence sufficiency on sc-c-best downgraded to insufficient. All Option C scenarios flagged for Exp: fetch Babylon.js docs.
